behavior_summary: A dual-clock FIFO with 17 storage entries of 8 bits each. Data is written on the push side when `push_valid` is asserted for a `push_clk` cycle and the FIFO accepts the write. Data is presented on the pop side with `pop_valid`/`pop_ready` handshake semantics; a dequeue occurs on a `pop_clk` edge when both are high. `pop_data` and `pop_valid` are combinationally derived rather than output-registered, so when the FIFO is empty and a push-side write becomes visible across the internal structure, the design may provide cut-through data to the pop side without an extra pop-side output register stage. The push side uses a credit-return scheme: each successfully consumed pop transaction eventually produces a one-cycle `push_credit` pulse in the push clock domain unless credit return is stalled by `push_credit_stall`. Push-side reset state is controlled by synchronous active-high `push_rst` and by `push_sender_in_reset`; write-side logic remains in reset while either is asserted, and `push_receiver_in_reset` reports that reset state. Read/write pointer and full/empty status communication between domains rely on 2-stage synchronization, so cross-domain status/credit visibility is delayed and should be treated as protocol-level behavior rather than exact-cycle arithmetic equivalence.
edge_cases: The FIFO has nominal physical depth 17 entries; occupancy-related values must remain bounded to the representable range 0 through 17.
If `pop_valid` is high and `pop_ready` is low, `pop_data` must remain stable and the item must not be dequeued until a later handshake occurs.

Because `pop_valid` and `pop_data` are not internally registered, empty-FIFO cut-through behavior is intended; however, the exact same-cycle visibility across the two domains is not fully specified and should not be asserted cycle-exactly.
Credits are returned in response to successful pop-side reads, but their appearance on `push_credit` is mediated by cross-domain synchronization and can be delayed.

If `push_credit_stall` is asserted, credit return can be paused; whether credits are deferred and later replayed or simply suppressed until unstalled is only partially specified at the interface level.

The push-side logic remains in reset while either `push_rst` or `push_sender_in_reset` is asserted; because resets are internally registered, externally visible reset effects are delayed by one local clock cycle.
The pop-side reset is also synchronous active-high with one-cycle delayed internal effect in the pop domain.
Full and empty indications depend on synchronized remote pointers, so transitions into or out of `push_full`/`pop_empty` can lag real activity by synchronizer delay.
The spec describes `credit_available_push` as `credit_count_push - credit_withhold_push`, but does not explicitly define saturation, signedness, or behavior if withhold exceeds count.
state_transitions: Push-domain reset state enters when `push_rst` or `push_sender_in_reset` is asserted and remains active while either stays high; observable effect occurs after the local reset-register delay.
Push-domain active state resumes after both `push_rst` and `push_sender_in_reset` are deasserted and the local reset-register delay has elapsed.
Pop-domain reset state enters when `pop_rst` is asserted; observable effect occurs after one `pop_clk` due to internal reset registration.
Pop-domain active state resumes after `pop_rst` is deasserted and the local reset-register delay has elapsed.
FIFO occupancy increases by one for each accepted push-side write.
FIFO occupancy decreases by one for each completed pop-side handshake (`pop_valid && pop_ready` on `pop_clk`).
`pop_empty` corresponds to zero readable items in the FIFO, subject to synchronization latency.
`push_full` corresponds to no writable space remaining, subject to synchronization latency.
Output-hold state on the pop side persists while `pop_valid=1` and `pop_ready=0`; transition out occurs when the handshake completes.
priorities: On the push side, reset condition (`push_rst` or `push_sender_in_reset`) overrides normal write-side operation.
On the pop side, `pop_rst` overrides normal read-side operation.
A pop transaction occurs only when both `pop_valid` and `pop_ready` are high; `pop_ready` alone has no effect when `pop_valid` is low.
While stalled (`push_credit_stall=1`), suppression/deferral of `push_credit` takes priority over immediate credit pulse generation.
For output data retention, holding current `pop_data` under `pop_valid && !pop_ready` takes priority over advancing to a next item.
mutation_risks: Reset-delay bug: implementing `push_rst`/`pop_rst` effects immediately instead of one local clock later.
Reset-handshake bug: ignoring `push_sender_in_reset`, or failing to keep write-side logic in reset while it remains asserted.
Incorrect polarity or synchronous/asynchronous handling of resets.
Producing `push_receiver_in_reset` from only `push_rst` instead of the combined write-side reset condition.
Returning a credit for every pop-ready pulse instead of only for successful `pop_valid && pop_ready` handshakes.
Dropping or duplicating credit pulses when `push_credit_stall` is asserted.
Wrong full/empty boundary at depth 17, especially off-by-one errors at occupancy 0, 16, or 17.
Incorrect width or wraparound of occupancy/slot/credit counters.
Advancing read state when `pop_valid=1` but `pop_ready=0`, causing data loss under backpressure.
Failing to hold `pop_data` stable under backpressure.
Incorrect FIFO ordering across multiple writes and reads.
Missing combinational cut-through when empty, or falsely claiming valid data when empty.
Using registered `pop_valid`/`pop_data` and introducing an unintended extra cycle of latency.
Improper synchronizer handling that causes status or credit outputs to update too early or in the wrong domain.
executable_reference: Treat the FIFO as an ordered queue of accepted writes and completed reads; for every completed pop handshake, the observed `pop_data` must equal the oldest not-yet-popped accepted `push_data` value.
A pop completes only on a `pop_clk` edge where `pop_valid` and `pop_ready` are both high.
When `pop_valid=1` and `pop_ready=0`, `pop_data` and the identity of the next item to be popped must remain unchanged until a later successful handshake or reset.
No item may be popped unless it was previously accepted on the push side.
No accepted item may be duplicated or skipped in pop order.

While the write side is in reset (`push_rst` or `push_sender_in_reset`, with the specified one-cycle local delay), writes must not be treated as accepted and `push_receiver_in_reset` must indicate reset state.
While the read side is in reset (`pop_rst`, with one-cycle local delay), no normal dequeue behavior should be required from the pop interface.
Each successful pop must cause at most one returned push-side credit pulse, and no credit pulse should occur without a corresponding prior successful pop.
Do not require a fixed-cycle latency from pop completion to `push_credit`; only require eventual one-for-one credit correspondence when `push_credit_stall` does not block return indefinitely.
Do not require exact same-cycle cut-through timing; only require that `pop_valid`/`pop_data` behavior remains consistent with FIFO ordering and backpressure rules.
guaranteed_properties: The module is a dual-clock FIFO connecting `push_clk` producer logic to `pop_clk` consumer logic.
The storage capacity is 17 entries and each stored data item is 8 bits wide.
Push-side writes are associated with `push_valid` in the `push_clk` domain.
Pop-side consumption occurs on `pop_clk` when `pop_valid` and `pop_ready` are both high.
If `pop_valid` is high and `pop_ready` is low, the same `pop_data` is held until handshake completion.
`push_rst` and `pop_rst` are active-high synchronous resets whose internal effect is delayed by one clock cycle in their respective domains.
The write-side logic stays in reset while either `push_rst` or `push_sender_in_reset` is asserted.
`push_receiver_in_reset` is asserted to indicate the FIFO's write-side reset state.
`push_credit` is a one-cycle pulse in the push clock domain corresponding to successful read-side consumption events.
`push_full` indicates no available space and `pop_empty` indicates no data available.
The design uses 2-stage synchronization of internal pointers between the two clock domains.
ambiguous_properties: The exact cycle latency from a push-side write to pop-side `pop_valid`/`pop_data` visibility, especially for cut-through when empty, is not fully specified.

The exact cycle latency from a successful pop to `push_credit` pulse assertion is not specified because credit return crosses clock domains and is described as combinationally generated from read-side activity.

The precise acceptance condition for a push when the sender uses credits is not explicitly stated through a `push_ready` handshake; it is implied that the sender should only drive valid when credits permit, but the FIFO-side behavior on an illegal push attempt is not explicitly defined.
Whether `push_valid` alone always guarantees a write, or whether writes may be internally blocked when full, is not spelled out explicitly.
The exact arithmetic semantics and update timing of `push_slots`, `credit_count_push`, `credit_available_push`, and `pop_items` are underspecified across reset boundaries and synchronizer delays.
The exact width of the count/status ports is not shown in the provided truncated RTL header; width 5 is the smallest reasonable inference for representing values 0 through 17.
The behavior of `credit_available_push` when `credit_withhold_push` exceeds `credit_count_push` is not defined.
The exact value of `push_full`, `pop_empty`, and count outputs during the one-cycle internally registered reset-delay window is not completely specified.
The spec does not provide a complete same-cycle truth table for simultaneous local activity and remotely synchronized pointer updates.
forbidden_assumptions: Do not assume zero-latency or a fixed latency for CDC propagation between push and pop domains.
Do not assume `push_credit` appears on a specific push clock cycle relative to a pop handshake.

Do not assume `pop_valid`, `pop_empty`, `push_full`, `push_slots`, `pop_items`, `credit_count_push`, or `credit_available_push` equal an exact instantaneous occupancy formula on every cycle across domains.

Do not assume `credit_available_push = credit_count_push - credit_withhold_push` is implemented with a particular saturation, wraparound, or signed arithmetic behavior unless explicitly observed in the RTL contract.
Do not assume an illegal `push_valid` while full is accepted or rejected in any specific way unless the interface contract or RTL makes that explicit.
Do not assume output count/status ports update in the same cycle as the causative push/pop event.
Do not assume cut-through means same-cycle pass-through from `push_valid/push_data` to `pop_valid/pop_data`; only the existence of a combinational unregistered path is stated.
Do not assume reset takes effect combinationally or asynchronously; the spec explicitly says synchronous active-high with one-cycle delayed internal effect.
Do not assume exact internal pointer encoding or synchronizer implementation details beyond the existence of 2-stage synchronization.
